content: I think having cargo on Linux would be pretty useful, as it will enable the developers to use the crates on crates.io, which is a bunch and would speed up development.
The upstream kernel developers have strong views on incorporating 3rd party code like that, so we're not going to attempt to use either cargo nor external crates for the time being.
Will you publish the Linux API bindings to crates.io?
not everyone wants to use make files
For modules
Rust analyzer doesn't understand makefiles

We don't have any plans to do so, no. make files are how the kernel is built.

Part of the goal of incorpating this into the kernel upstream is adhering to kernel norms.

Tiago Dinis said:

Rust analyzer doesn't understand makefiles

there is a make target to create rust-project.json used by rust-analyzer, it works for me
just run make rust-analyzer

Tiago Dinis said:

Will you publish the Linux API bindings to crates.io?

how would you use the binding without the C components? I do not see how we would ship the entire kernel over crates...
I mean the module apis
what advantage would that give you?
module APIs are built to a particular kernel, depending on build config. you need to get them with the kernel itself
I see cargo more so useful for out-of-tree modules
What functionality of cargo would you want to use?

Giving the developer the ability to write kernel modules with third party dependencies for one. Given they’re out of tree, the module developer could incorporate whatever crate-compatible with the linux kernel.

In that regards having the kernel crate bindings would be really useful.

Giving the developer the ability to write kernel modules with third party dependencies for one.

Developers can still do it, we are not removing the ability to do that.

In that regards having the kernel crate bindings would be really useful.

I am not sure I understand what you mean. Out-of-tree modules have access to the kernel crate (as long as licenses are respected).
Was referring it to be on crates.io
Realized I was conflating crates with cargo I am essentially advocating the same as OP by having the kernel crate available as pullable dependency on crates.io
What you are actually asking, I think, is to make the Rust support independent from the kernel, so that out-of-tree modules can use the kernel crate without a Rust-enabled kernel.

having the kernel crate available as pullable dependency on crates.io

Again, I am not sure what you mean by this. Even if we were to use crates.io (very unlikely in the short-term), the kernel abstractions are tied to a particular kernel and are intended to be developed within the same tree as the kernel.

If someone wants to maintain some abstractions independently of the kernel tree and maybe even making them capable of targeting different kernel versions with them etc., they are free to do so as long as they respect the license.

Miguel Ojeda said:

What you are actually asking, I think, is to make the Rust support independent from the kernel, so that out-of-tree modules can use the kernel crate without a Rust-enabled kernel.

Yes, this is what I'm asking for, but as you mentioned it maybe out of scope for this project.

Please note that kernel internal APIs are not stable at all, thus doing so would entail a huge amount of work. And yes, some companies may do so internally like that, and it is fine as long as they respect the rules, but that is their call. In general, it is easier if you mainline your code.

Baye Dieng said:

Yes, this is what I'm asking for, but as you mentioned it maybe out of scope for this project.

But that is slightly different, orthogonal to the build system.

Having kernel modules written in Rust without a Rust-enabled kernel is possible, and in fact quite a few demo projects did precisely that over the years. But of course, if you develop the abstractions from outside the kernel tree, you will arrive at the same problem as above.
Yes, though I was making this assertion based on the premise that rust would already be enabled within the kernel.
This was precisely one of goals behind Rust for Linux: to make it possible to develop the abstractions in-tree and get them properly supported (plus make everything work smoothly, of course).

Baye Dieng said:

Yes, though I was making this assertion based on the premise that rust would already be enabled within the kernel.

If Rust is enabled in the kernel, then you already have access to the abstractions.

Is there already (or is there something willing to write) documentation that is "Things to know about kernel development, if you're familiar with Rust development" and similarly "Things to know about Rust, if you're familiar with kernel development"? It seems like this topic (cargo), and other topics (fallible allocation) would be good stuff to cover.

Ted has also suggested us to provide similar documentation (in a short and to the point format, if I understood him correctly), at least for kernel developers. I will try to provide it, at least in that "direction".

I only looked a few times at user crates, but every time I did there were liberal uses of allocations (Arc::new, Box::new, Vec::push, etc.) that would result in panics on allocation failures.

So using crates from crates.io in the kernel directly is too risky.

That isn't to say that I oppose sharing. I'd really like it. Unfortunately the current state of affairs isn't conductive of that. Perhaps it's time to start changing this.

For build dependencies using allocations and having potential panics all over the place is fine. For those this reason to not use crates.io doesn't apply. As for actual runtime dependencies, I do agree that we have to at the very least carefully vet them for doing things that are not ok in kernel space.

bjorn3 said:

For build dependencies using allocations and having potential panics all over the place is fine. For those this reason to not use crates.io doesn't apply. As for actual runtime dependencies, I do agree that we have to at the very least carefully vet them for doing things that are not ok in kernel space.

@Gary Guo had a linter to detect allocations, right? Perhaps we could use that as part of vetting crates...
We have removed the nonfallible allocation from liballoc so crates using those would straight-out not compile.
For detecting any allocation, not just nonfallible allocation, compiling with --extern alloc=/path/to/empty_crate.rlib --extern std=/path/to/empty_crate.rlib would work I think.

Wedson Almeida Filho said:

I only looked a few times at user crates, but every time I did there were liberal uses of allocations (Arc::new, Box::new, Vec::push, etc.) that would result in panics on allocation failures.

I imagine one of the first items in the "kernel programming for Rust devs" doc would be, "You have to use try_new not new for things that allocate".

"Can I use Vec in the kernel?" was literally the first question one friend of mine asked after seeing the initial Rust supported landed. :P

FWIW because of the lack of sufficient APIs in rust, it's very hard to even _write_ 3p crate that supports fallible allocation yet. https://github.com/alex/rust-asn1/commit/2df705301b15666f5f6e5061f539bc4d272c25a8 is what I did for rust-asn1, but its reserve+infallible, which fails the linter for obvious reasons.
